original: You step closer to the large tent. On the way, you spot a couple of tracks done by very different kinds of creatures. They are very close to one another, certainly colleagues or hunters. There’s a set of plantigrade ones about the same size as a trail of avian, taloned feet. Next to them, enormous, digitigrade footprints sink deep into the snow. You aren’t an expert in uvetan fauna, but it looks big enough to be a very large creature. You hope it’s not carnivorous.

Small pebbles taken from the lake protect the shelter’s many supporting pegs. The howling winds try relentlessly to tear the fabric apart, but the sturdy structure doesn’t show any sign of flinching. Looking at it more closely, you identify a small, hardlight mesh woven on top of the flexible tissue. A faint warmth radiates from it, like a pleasant caress that beckons you to enter. Before you can do so, an imperious voice booms from the inside.
“Shake the snow out before entering, would you? I’d like my doormat to stay dry for once.”
You shrug, casually dusting the cool snowflakes off you
Not willing to infuriate your host, you dutifully dust yourself off the worst of the snow
You shrug, removing the worst of the icy flakes
You barely dust yourself|You spin around, making the little snowflakes on you fly up

before stepping into the controlled habitat. Your face brightens as soon as you walk into the large, circular room, a stove in the center spreading a fiendish warmth all around the segmented shelter. You could easily cook a small feast with it, but its main function seems to be repelling the arctic cold. A blue, velvety couch and a couple of sofas line the artificial fireplace. A big pile of pillows and blankets takes a good chunk of the left side - certainly the beast’s bed. Three layers of rugs and cloth cover the floor, barring the cold from sneaking in through the soil.

On the left lies a smaller chamber filled to the brim with food and supplies. A couple of indigenous species happily swim in a couple of observation tanks: crabs, silverfish-like crustaceans, and bioluminescent fishes you’ve never seen before.

More concerningly, a huge "+(j.dI.entryUnlocked("Lurelings")?"lureling":"anglerfish-like thing")+" snoozes in a reinforced, pressurized tank. It blinks at you a couple of times before resuming its nap, unshaken by your presence.

Before you can scan the place more, a tall anat female joins you from the smaller room on the right. “Greetings. You must be terribly cold, aren’t you? Please, take a seat and make yourself comfortable.”
And she is?
The amber-feathered avian blushes. “My apologies, I don’t have many visitors so my manners are a bit rusty. I’m Dahlia and this is my little nest. May I know your name, [pc.mf|handsome|cutie]?”
“[pc.name],” you simply answer.

She smiles. “Well, what brings you to my outpost, [pc.name]? Let me guess... you are a savicite prospector. No, you look more like an adventurer... or maybe a tourist looking for a brutal, primal mate? You wouldn’t believe how many sex fiends become milodan [pc.mf|boytoys|broodmothers] after an eventful weekend on our lovely moon,” she says, a bright fire in her chestnut eyes.
You shrug. “And what are you doing here?”
Dahlia recovers a teapot from her stove and serves you a mug of fuming tea before taking her place on a sofa. “Surveying the wildlife. Not a job one can do from a desk in Uveto station.”
That’s not the easiest job. It must be hard to live here, away from the city, exposed to the moon’s unforgiving environment.

The amber bird takes a sip of searing hot tea. “It’s not all bad. I’ve pretty flexible work hours, very good pay, and almost all my expenses are covered. I also get to meet some interesting people. The icy plains are full of surprises,” she says before checking her watch. “Speaking of, we shouldn’t be alone for lo-”
Next

She doesn’t have the time to finish her sentence before a hulking, bear-like creature surges into the tent. It shakes many layers of snow off his russet fur - all over the carpets - then lazily collapses on the pillow pile, not bothered in the slightest by your presence. A moment later, a very plush figure follows suit. She’s slightly shorter than Dahlia, but a lot wider, especially stuffed in her polar coat. Nine white-furred tails with violet tips sprout from her back and perky, vulpine ears frame her round face. She’s blushing so brightly, her ebony skin looks as red as a tomato.
“Guggoth no! Bad boy! I’m so sorry Dahlia, he left snow all over the carpet again.”
Your host sighs then taps her lap. “No worries. Come here, sweetie, we’ve got a lovely guest.”

The overweight essyra blushes even deeper. She slowly waddles to Dahlia’s side, babbling incomprehensible words through her juicy lips. As soon as Dahlia’s fingers touch the flustered girl’s ears, she immediately relaxes.
“Feeling better, hon?”
“Yes,” the fox girl mutters. “Who’s our new friend?”
“[pc.name],” you answer. “We were chatting a little before you arrived, Miss...?”
“S-Sloan. My name is Sloan. N-Nice to meet you, [pc.name].”
Dahlia ruffles the big woman’s hair. “It’s always frightening to see you [silly|fold like origami|become a blushing violet] whenever you’re in public. Where did my big, tough huntress go?”
“Hunting easy, words hard,” she grumbles back.
Her big, tough huntress? Are they married?

“Indeed. Sloan’s keeping me safe and helps me capture interesting specimens. She also keeps me warm through uveto’s cold, gloomy nights,” she teases. “You can stay here as long as you want. We’ll make your stay as enjoyable as possible. We can even share some body warmth, if you need.”
“Dahlia! We barely know [pc.himHer]! You can’t just share our bed with strangers like this.”

The anat girl reaches for her mug and takes a large gulp of tea. “Sorry, I got a little carried away. Is there anything we can do for you, [pc.name]? We’ve got spare arctic gear, if you need help crossing the tundra. Sloan can cook you a fine meal with our homemade goods, or we can talk about the wonderful ecosystem of my beloved homeworld. If there’s one thing we Uvetan are good at, it’s being charming hosts.”
Is there something else you’d like to do with Dahlia and Sloan?

You head to Dahlia’s huge, segmented tent. Despite how hard the wind blows, it still stands defiantly against the elements. On the way, the anat researcher leaves her refuge, a cumbersome scanner in hand. She observes the readings and her surroundings for a moment before noticing you. She makes ample moves with her arms, fighting the merciless gale to urge you to get to safety. You quickly cross the last few yards between you and the warm, cozy shelter.

“Welcome back, [pc.name]. Let me just-” she says, [pc.isNude|wrapping you with a wooly blanket. “Better. I wouldn’t want my favorite [pc.raceShort] to freeze solid. Please, come in, I just put some water to boil.”|dusting some snow off your shoulders[pc.isTaur| and tauric lower body]. “Better. As much as I love the snow, I’d rather it stays outside of my home. Please come in, I just put some water to boil.”]

The place is as lovely as you remember. It’s filled with soft sofas, colorful rugs, a furnished kitchen, and a couple of tanks filled with indigenous specimens.[silly| Inside the largest tank, the old lureling shivers. The psionic beast stares at you for a couple of seconds before resuming his nap.] As soon as Sloan moves to greet you, her russet-furred beast rises from a pile of blankets and begs her for belly rubs like a tiny puppy.
“Guggoth please, we’ve got a guest.”

Dahlia giggles. “Welcome to our little nest, [pc.name]. Please excuse the disorder, I’ve been so busy working on these beautiful anormalocaris’ mating parades,” she says, looking at silverfish-like creatures swirling in a clear aquarium.

The athletic girl casually guides you to the velvety furniture and offers you a warm drink. Sloan’s breasts and ass bounce wildly as she hops to her wife’s side. You take your ease on the couch while the two lovebirds lean on each other. The bird lady slowly caresses her wife’s fuzzy ears and rubs her belly through her clothes. It’s worrying how fast the skilled huntress becomes a weak prey in her [silly|roommate|spouse]’s hands.
“What can we do for you, [pc.name]?”

“Us? There’s a lot to say. Where to start?”
“M-Maybe our first date,” the essyra mumbles, almost melting on the spot. Their first time must have been wonderful, if its memory alone flusters her so much.

Dahlia shakes her head. “It was a few years back. I just finished my degree in xenobiology from Ausaril Tech... I can’t believe I managed to get a grant for the number one ausar institute of technology.”
“It’s because you are the best[silly|est],” Sloan murmurs, cheeks as bright as a tomato.

“Maybe. So, I was preparing for my first field trip and the yearly Irestead fair had just begun. After work, I took my old ice skating gear and went to the ice ring. I was a bit rusty, but I still managed to make a few swirls and leaps. All was going well until a beginner bumped into me. I caught the poor thing a moment before she hit the ground.”
Sloan’s ears perk up. “My balance was so bad. How many times have I apologized for that?”

“Exactly twenty-five times. I shut her up with my index finger, then offered her a hand. When I saw just how little experience she had, I gave her a first lesson. She was a pretty good student, very involved. We then spent the evening and night together at the fair and we never truly separated afterward.”
That’s a lot cuter than you expected it to be. No “lone avian lost in a blizzard and rescued by a curious fox” story.

“I know better than to get lost in a snowstorm. Although, I wouldn’t be against being rescued by a big, plush essyra. She could wrap me up in her tails and share her body warmth with me,” she teases, passing a hand through the overweight lady’s tail fluff. “I’d love to be pampered by a strong huntress with big, chocolate boobs and thighs as soft as a down-stuffed pillow.”

“I-I won’t fall for that again,” the dark-skinned essyra pouts. “I was genuinely worried about you! Skilled trekkers disappear each year in the tundra, never to be seen again. I almost had a stroke when I received your distress signal! And I almost had another when I found you in your tent, waiting for me, naked on your bunk, and your eyes... staring at me like an alpha Hcep about to swoop an adult kor’diiak.”
Dahlia takes a sip of warm tea. “The patient hunter gets the prey.”
Speaking of hunting, you don’t know much about the shy essyra. Can she tell you more about herself?

Sloan slowly nods. “There’s not much to say. I’m an essyra born in a village on the other side of Uveto, raised with her sisters, who became a huntress like many others of her kin since the dawn of time. Nothing fancy, really.”

Dahlia sighs. “Come on, hon. Your family was the first to be uplifted. It’s their knowledge of the ice that allowed the creation of the underground railway. Heck, your mom spliced cold-resistant crops with the genes of an aquatic fern. And you certainly are the best pathfinder and interceptor of your generation.”
The big vulpine blushes harder than ever. If she were a boiler, she’d be near the meltdown point. “I-I... no. I’m just doing normal stuff every other huntress does.”

“Like raising an orphaned kor’diiak from infancy to adulthood, rather than taming an adult one? Or using psionically-attuned lures to distract lurelings from vulnerable prey? No, there’s only one arctic fox brave and bold enough to try something so dangerous and live to tell the story,” the amber-feathered lady remarks. “And besides, do you truly think I’d marry a garden-variety essyra?”
She doesn’t look like one at all, if it can reassure her. They go well together; no wonder they married and settled together.

“Yeah, I tied her knot a couple of times already,” Dahlia teases, making her love shiver. Taking advantage of her flustered state, she reaches for a small holopad and scrolls through her files. The avian hands you a bright picture of them in wedding attire. Dahlia is wearing a Stormguard-branded dress that looks a lot like ancient terran outfits. Sloan’s body, on the other hand, is adorned with trophies, bones, and jewels, her skin painted with tribal swirls and symbols. She looks like an ancient spirit that lured a poor maiden to her fluffy grasp.
They look beautiful.

“I know, right? I still can’t believe mom let me borrow grandma’s dress. Sloan wanted a simple dress too, but as a huntress, she had to be in traditional garb. You know, she got each of these trophies herself, using nothing but an old, wooden spear and an obsidian knife. Her headdress and shoulders come from a blorgg prime - a blind megalodon with a huge segmented mouth! Its arm-length fangs and plated bones made a magnificent diadem and corset. [silly|Wotan’s beard|Stars], I’m so lucky to have a warrior queen all to myself.”

The plush fox girl frowns. “I don’t like fighting. Also, I looked better in my dancing gown,” she murmurs. She then gently slides to the next picture. Dahlia is still in her classic dress, but the plump vulpine has changed into a bright red dress woven with amethysts. “We danced all night long. Your dad had to stop us to slice the cake.”
“Yeah. My old man’s a typical anat guy. Always an eye on the clock. [silly|Freya’s tits|Stars], I could use some of mom’s homemade jam-filled rolls.”
“Me too,” Sloan replies, “especially if the biscuit is gorged with cherry liquor. I love it when it’s extra spongy. Maybe I can bring some back the next time I shop in Irestead.”
Speaking of Irestead. Why are they living so far from the city?

Dahlia finishes her mug in one big gulp before answering. “I’m a xenobiologist specialized in wildlife surveillance and growth. My task is to study the frozen lakes’ ecosystems and determine the optimal equilibrium to sustain the largest number of various specimens. As for Sloan, she’s paid to keep the main roads safe from beasts and to hunt down aggressive specimens that can’t be handled otherwise. My job covers most of my expenses since I’m out here in the field, [silly|fighting for my meal|exposed to the elements]. We made a lot out of this old XXL Akkadi Arctic Expedition Tent. It’s ancient tech, but the heating system and hardlight shielding are pretty resilient and easy to maintain. The only problem is that we went overboard and furnished it with so many things. We’ll need to borrow a stormguard shuttle to move all our stuff.”

“Please Dally, your mom will requisition it on the double and your little brother will be in the pilot’s seat in a heartbeat. If you have a cold, the Stormguard will be here to bring you medicine and zhor beast soup - even the old Sigvald will crawl out of his cloister to help you.”
They seem to have a peaceful but busy life.
“Indeed. Now, what to do with our guest? I’ve got a couple positio- I mean ideas I’d like to try.”
“You are irredeemable,” Sloan sighs.

textify(Tn||(Tn=(0,C.Z)(["“Standing out here in the wild, waiting for prey to fall into our carefully laid trap.” “Dahlia please, you’ll scare [pc.name] off. [pc.HeShe] will think we are nothing but sex fiends,” Sloan pouts. If they aren’t sex-crazed outcasts, what are they doing for a living? The athletic avian rises from the couch and walks toward a couple of aquariums filled with indigenous fishes, crustaceans, and batrachians. “I’m a xenobiologist. I’m here to survey the evolution of the fauna and flora around the frozen lakes. Our goal is to optimize the variety and growth of the current population without significantly changing the ecosystem. We’ve found out that the bigger anormalocaris and rimetrouts are, the more diverse a frozen body of water becomes,” she says, glancing at a silverfish-like crustacean gently floating in its tank. “It’s no surprise that in an arctic environment, most resources come from the rivers and oceans. Sometimes I wish I could join the undersea research department, to study the strange creatures roaming the glacial depths.”"])))
You’ll pass on this one. The giant electric jellyfish that attacked you in the Deep Sea research facility was enough of a cautionary tale.
She’s a strange one. Her job allows for work inside, protected from the elements, and yet she’s happy to be at the mercy of this moon’s devastating thunderstorms and blizzard.

"+textify(In||(In=(0,C.Z)(["The amber-feathered scientist shrugs. “There’s only so much automated drones can do. Compared to the meteorology center, we have little to no ressources for replacing broken instruments. A strong tempest will wreck robots worth more than my yearly income, but a skilled field agent can endure, especially if she’s got two big, meaty bodyguards,” she says, giving the russet kor’diiak some belly rubs. The humongous beast barks happily, like a small puppy. “Go slow, Dahlia, Guggoth’s been disobeying me a lot lately. I don’t want to reward his bad behavior.” The bird lady shakes her head. “It’s just a bit of snow on the carpet, that’s not a big deal. How can you be so cruel to deny pats for that?” Sloan pouts. “Cruel? You’re the one that berates me when ONE snowflake falls from my tails and melts on your soil.” “The difference between you and him, dear, is that you are the one sleeping with me. I like my furs warm and dry, not wet and soggy.” Maybe you should intervene before they start a cat fight. Speaking of furs, do they come from the essyra’s prey? The shy lady suddenly builds some courage; you can see pride in her eyes. “Yes and no. A lot of mammals on Uveto shed their wool and fur when they grow older, so it’s easy to harvest raw materials from them to make rugs, blankets, clothes, and so on. Walruses, kor’diiaks, giant sky rays, and veiny eels provide good sources of food. If you take one down, you can have meat for an entire trimester - assuming you can salt, steam, or pickle it of course.” Are there specific techniques essyra huntresses use? Sloan shakes her head. “Not anymore, no. We used to fish with our tails and lay primitive traps to catch small frys, but we don’t use them anymore. We still learn the old ways in case we end up isolated from one another and desperately need to eat. As rewarding as drawing a homemade bow feels, I’d rather use an energy rifle or an old carbine. Don’t get me wrong, I’m not an anti-tradition protester. I still use a spear to threaten large predators like my ancestors did, but I mostly strike with the side equipped with the taser goad. A stun rifle also does wonders on a target. It keeps the meat, fur, down, and scales in great shape.” Does she hunt a lot of prey? “No. We essyra used to be prey as well. There’s a reason we breed true and are short-lived without genetic uplifting. Before the U.G.C settled our moon, even the weakest lurelings could have a tribe of marion at their beck and call. I can’t imagine being naught but a manipulative monster’s feeding slave.” Dahlia raises an eyebrow. “Oh? So I’m finally getting an oily massage?” The vulpine huntress pouts. “You know what I mean, Dahlia. You are nothing like the lureling dominators of the past. They would pimp my people to milodan mating ceremonies and korgonne packs just for their amusement. And no, I’m not buying coconut oil just to give you a happy ending.” They look quite open about their sexuality. Dahlia joins Sloan on the couch and reaches for her mug. “Indeed. We agreed in our vows to have a little freedom concerning our respective needs. As much as I love my sweet, plump love, I’m always looking for new pre- uh, partners, new partners. The subbier the merrier,” she whispers, licking her avian chops. “I’d love to add "," to my collection. I wonder how they yelp and moan.” “Hon please, you’ll scare [pc.himHer] off!” The feathered researcher smirks. “If you want a more obedient and kind lay, I know a big fluffy girl. I’ve been told she loves being tied with her own tails and teased for hours. Some bold souls tried to take us both at the same time and lost many hours of their lives to intense, debilitating lovemaking. We don’t have a lot of visitors, so I tend to give it my all to make things memorable.” [silly|Don’t they fear they’ll over-excite toad in the tank? you ask, glancing at the snoozing lureling rolling in his tank. “This fellow is old, very old, possibly over a hundred years,” Sloan replies, her eyes nothing but an ocean of compassion. “His psionic aura is so weak. I need to have forehead-to-antenna contact to hear him. We found him unconscious, floating on the lake’s surface. No matter how much damage his species has done to mine, I couldn’t let him starve to death.” “Don’t worry, [pc.name]. Sloan’s immune to psionic control, her entire family is. The lureling isn’t talking through her. Our intense cuddling suffices to keep him well fed - that and malt balls. Drop one in his tank and you’ll be his best friend. I feel a bit bad to keep him like this, but he’s an excellent test subject - and he gives me new ideas to top Sloan :p”]"])),M.m.indefiniteArticle(ParseText("[pc.race]")))
“You wanna make a throuple without buying me dinner first? That’s quite bold of you,” Dahlia mockingly winks.
“Hon, you are such a horndog,” Sloan sighs. “We are a happy, married couple. Not with child yet but we’ll get to it eventually,” she mumbles, becoming redder as she exposes her plans.
Dahlia nods. “One day, sweetie. Unfortunately, I can’t get pregnant without fertility boosters. My anat genes are too weak to reproduce with another species without help.”
Anats aren’t native to this moon. How did she end up here?

Dahlia takes a sip of herbal tea. “It might surprise you, but I was born here. My father is from the Anat Empire. He’s working as a logistic agent with the Camarilla to secure savicite exports to Caphist, his homeworld. As for my mother, she’s a human with modded wings. Her family participated in the foundation of the Stormguard back in the tenth Planet Rush. They met in a diplomatic mission with the korgonne that ended poorly. A couple of months later, they were engaged and Dad started the genetic therapy needed to crossbreed with humans. Long story short, a year later, I was born. Luckily, I inherited a lot from my mother’s side, stuff anat women don’t usually have like hair and nipples. They were a bit shocked that I wasn’t born with blue eyes like them, but my great-grandmother on my father’s side had hazel eyes so it wasn’t a big deal. Troubles came later.”
Troubles? What happened?

“In retrospect, it sounds silly, but it was a big drama in my family. When I was four, Mother got pregnant again. Dad wasn’t supposed to still be fertile at the time, so he accused her of cheating on him. They bickered a lot, and it became worse the closer she was to the term. Eventually, she gave birth to a half-anat boy that had the feathers and the eyes of Father. Unfortunately, the seeds they sow already germinated and the things they said to each other couldn’t be so easily forgotten. Poor Grimald had it rougher than I, especially since Mom wanted him to be a Stormguard warrior. My brother was too kind and pure to be a frontliner.”
You’ve met him before; he is indeed very sweet and passionate. It’s true that he’s a bit too frail to be a warrior, but you wouldn’t call him weak.
That sounds rough. You hope their childhood wasn’t too dire.

“I was lucky. School and sports were easy for me, so I quickly became the prodigy child of the family, not that it left Grimald off the hook. With an anat father and a stormguard mother, let’s just say that everything was a challenge to be overcome. I excelled in high school and since I fared well, I got a scholarship to study at Ausaril Tech. My college years were among the best of my life. I finally had some freedom and, for once, I wasn’t the top bitch in everything. I also partied a lot: men, women, herms... there’s nothing I didn’t taste. There was this PhD student I looked up to, an ausar girl who went to all the parties. She was a real sex fiend and a brilliant mind. She worked for Akkadi for a couple of years then left for a smaller company. Last I heard about her, she’d moved to a recently-discovered planet. If it weren’t for her, I wouldn’t know half my kinks. Anyway, with my degree in hand, I came back to Uveto and got a job as a xenobiologist. First, I upgraded the survey probes, then I became a field agent when I met Sloan.”
“You know, I could have settled with you in Irestead. You didn’t need to follow me in the wilds,” the fat essyra murmurs.
The half-anat shrugs. “Life doesn’t have the same thrill in Irestead. And besides, I don’t think our neighbors would tolerate our intense cuddling sessions.”
What’s Sloan story?
“I’m just an essyra huntress.”
“A pretty good essyra huntress,” Dahlia remarks. “Did you know she’s got a dragon sire?”
Wait really?
“N-No! Mama’s a gryvain, not a Frostwyrm!”
“Hon, I’ve been with gryvains, none had your girth.”

Sloan sighs. “Listen, I know there’s stories about my ancestors being graced by a benevolent Frostwyrm to fight lurelings, but these are nothing but legends. My kin mixed with lots of gryvain, that’s true, and many followed them across the stars, but that’s it.”
Does she have sisters?

“Many. My mothers made sure they’d have a large family, so they impregnated each other. They gave birth to me and to my nine siblings the same day. My sisters are all tall, strong amazons with draconic features they either inherited or modded themselves with.”
What’s her gryvain features?
The huntress’s cheeks turn crimson. “I... that’s a little personal.”
You see. She said her sisters were all muscle-bound ladies, is she the only plus-sized one?
Dahlia’s hands settle on the ebony fox’s shoulders. “You don’t have to talk about it.”

“It’s fine. A few years ago, I used to be as bulky and shredded as my siblings - a [silly|kitsune|vulpine] bodybuilder without a hint of fat. Truth be told, no matter how good people said I looked, I felt disgusted with myself. Seeing myself in a mirror, getting praised for my chiseled body, being lusted over by men and women for my gains, all these things made me feel terrible. I felt like I didn’t belong in my own flesh.”

“Well, [silly|I’m built different|I’m a weird gal],” Sloan murmurs.
Did Dahlia help her get her dream body?
The half-anat shakes her head. “Unfortunately no. She had to go through it alone.”

“Seeing my unease, my essyra mother told me to head south and fish as many coolcods, iceberg eels, and frost lobsters as possible. It was about that time I found Guggoth. The poor thing was but a little furball at the time, a russet, famished furball. I spent two months there, gorging him and myself on the fattest seafood our glacial waters can provide. Rainbow mussels and zhor beast were also on the menu when I could get my hands on them. It was a nice change of pace from all the rich fish steaks. My new proportions made quite an impression on my family, but it was for the best.”

“A few days later, we went to the yearly Irestead fair. I wanted to taste some honey candies but my sisters forcefully brought me with them to the ice ring. I was never good at skating, and that was before I bulked up. I fell off balance a couple of times. It really hurt, even with my extra softness. The third time was the charm, since the junior ice skating champion stopped my fall,” she says, turning her eyes on her spouse.
Dahlia hugs her lover close. “That’s now how you spell your Goddess.”

textify(Yn||(Yn=(0,C.Z)(["“I’d love to taste some of your cooking. I’m a little hungry and I could use something warm and hot before heading back outside.” Sloan frowns. “I-I don’t know. textify(Sn||(Sn=(0,C.Z)(["“I would like to eat some of your homemade cooking,” you say. “Your plates are so generous and so tasty.” Sloan blushes deeply. “T-Thank you. She rummages through a couple of bags and crates, gathering a couple of meaty sausages and slabs of meat. Even from here, you can see them shine with fat. About a minute later, she comes back with a large platter filled with pickled vegetables, herbs, potatoes, and meats in one hand and a bottle branded with the Stormguard’s emblem - certainly some kind of mead or wine. She places her bounty on a table near the electric stove then arranges a pan and a large casserole on the induction plates. “Dahlia? [pc.name]? Can you please peel the potatoes and cut them into fries while the yak fat is heating?” she asks, turning the burners and fryer on. “Sure, hon. What’s on the menu?” The vulpine huntress puts on a large apron before dropping fat slabs of pickled fish into the casserole. “Boreal bream with pickled turnip and carrots, stewed in [silly|mirrorwine|ice grape wine] served on a bed of seal steak, smoked sausages, and fries. Nothing fancy,” she enumerates, casually slicing onions, mincing herbs, and seasoning her main dish. That sounds incredibly fancy for a dish she came up with a minute ago. You follow Dahlia to the cutting board and take a small knife.
Briget and Vic made sure you’d know how to cook by yourself, so preparing potatoes for three is right up your alley. Dahlia, on the other hand, struggles to make regular cuts.
Briget showed you how to prepare vegetables, but you are far from as good as she was. Fortunately, Dahlia also struggles with her potatoes.
About halfway through, Sloan quickly checks on your work, smiles proudly, then goes to the stove.

Next to you, the russet kor’diiak bear huffs the fumes. The tamed beast trots like a puppy toward his mistress and rubs his muzzle against Sloan’s side. Dahlia reaches for an extra fat slab of meat and waves it at Guggoth. “Come here, big boy.”

The fluffy animal slowly approaches the half-anat, a hungry light in his eyes. He sits next to her, resting his head on her lap. “Yes, you are my good boy. My big, fat boy. If you were a bit fatter, you’d make a delicious roast,” she teases, earning an angry groan from the russet ursine. “Hey! I was joking!”
Did they have kor’diiak meat in the past?

“Twice,” Sloan whispers. “It’s very sinewy so you need to marinate it for months for it to be edible, but it’s a refined meat fit for banquets and special events. I haven’t had any since I adopted Guggoth, though. It took me some time to train this big glutton... time and lots of seal steaks.”

Dahlia feeds the largest piece of meat to the alien bear. With his prize in mouth, Guggoth returns to his comfy pile of pillows to savor his dish. Meanwhile, Sloan recovers the fries and plunges them into the hot fryer before putting the remaining steaks and some thinly-cut sausages into the pan. “I’ll take it from here. Can you please set the table? It’ll be ready in five minutes.”

You follow the half-anat back to the couch. She serves you a warm mug of herbal tea then recovers three sets of plates and cutlery. The plates are very wide, big enough to fit one of Guggoth’s steaks! They didn’t lie about making you a small feast. Minutes later, Sloan moves the fuming pots on the table and a basket filled with golden fries. She places a large slab of seal meat on your plate, lines it with sausage bits, adds a large ladle of stewed bream, turnip, and carrots on top with lots of sauce, and fills the rest of the plate with potatoes. The smell alone is gorgeous. You can’t wait to dig in!

Oh, right, you wander Uveto.”
<i>“That I do.
Rights, you free on Uveto.”

,” you quietly mumble to yourself.
You see his huge bow on the ground next to him, but he doesn’t seem to be hunting anything at the moment: he’d be hard-pressed to pursue prey from that position. You ask what he’s doing.

.” The old man gets up and grabs his bow, along with a quiver of large korgonne arrows, “"+(korgiTranslate()?"What are you up to":"What up to")+"?”

in your travels you must get in many fights, are you hurt or maybe tired
in travels must get in many fights, you hurt or tired

.” He dusts you off some before standing up and offering your hand to help you up as well. “"+(korgiTranslate()?"I’m very sorry":"Much sorry")+",” he says rubbing the back of his neck in embarrassment,his tongue poking out of his thin lips.

The sound of crunching snow nearby brings you back from the brink of unconsciousness. You try to see what awaits you, but the pain is too great. You feel yourself being dragged through the snow, the chill setting in harshly, quickly making you numb to sensation and only vaguely aware of the hand gripping your arm, pulling you along.

Trying to open your eyes takes some effort as the freezing wind gives no reprieve, forcing itself into your eyes as soon as you give any opening. The seeping energy of the cold makes any effort exhausting, and soon you’re forced to give up, your eyes closing as your body goes limp once more.

original: The chimera behind the desk closes the monitor in front of him as you approach. He’s wearing a Steele Tech standard-issue lab coat, worn open to reveal thick orange fur across his chest and shoulders almost like a mantle, as well as around his wrists, matching the luxurious mane that cascades down the center of his back and is parted at his forehead by a pair of massive black bull-like horns. Below the thicker orange fluff, thinner white fur covers the rest of his upper body, the same shade as the feathers of his rather enormous avian wings. Behind him you can see the signature coils of a naga’s tail, black scales with a white underbelly. As if all that wasn’t enough to make him absolutely monstrous in appearance, now that you’re closer you can also see that he has four arms, three of them resting at the desk even as the fourth is raised in greeting.

“Ah, welcome. I am Doctor Edward Lessau. Might I have the pleasure of your name, "+pc.mf("Mister","Miss")+"...?” His tone is surprisingly cultured for someone with such a savage appearance. You’d expect this kind of voice out of the guests at your dad’s formal dinner parties, not someone who looks like a creature you’d see on one of Steph Irson’s shows.
“Steele, [pc.name] Steele,” you reply. Lessau’s slitted eyes light up at the name; not just figuratively, you notice.
“Ah, [pc.name]! It’s a pleasure to meet you at last.” The chimera’s smile turns a mixture of warm and teasing.
“Making good use of my masterwork, I see.”
“Not keen to follow in your father’s footsteps with regards to transformations, I see? Or perhaps you’re just looking for the right species to make proper use of that masterpiece of mine.”
“The hell do you mean by that?” you ask.
Your surprise is visible enough that Lessau sees it and laughs.

“You didn’t think those microsurgeons that protect you were conjured from thin air, did you? They were created specially for you, twenty years in the making. This may be the first time we’ve met, Captain Steele, but we have each done great things for the other. Without you, and without your father, I would not be here today.”
The chimera looks as though he’s about to say more, but shakes his head after a moment.

“Well, I imagine you didn’t come in for a reminder of your recent loss. My apologies. Come, take a look. I have quite a number of transformatives that might interest you, if you yearn to become something truly exotic. We can prepare them for you right here.”

“So what’s the head of Steele Tech’s biomedical division doing in a backwater like this?” you ask.

“Ah, yes, that. This planet, as I’m sure you’re aware, is lush with psionically gifted lifeforms owing to the abundant savicite. Other such planets exist, but Uveto is one of the few with such diversity and genetic closeness to the coreworld races. It presents a treasure trove of potential advancements in adapting those gifts to humans or ausar. Such a breakthrough would far surpass any progress that Xenogen has made on that front.”

“It also gives us an opportunity to test treatments for environmental adaptation. The local ausar have of course modified themselves to an extent, but there’s potential for far more. Some of the employees have expressed interest in gaining the physical traits of the ice chimeras, not to mention the more humanoid races...” Lessau trails off, noticing he’s begun to ramble.
“In essence, Captain, there’s dozens of reasons to be here. When we’ve unraveled enough of this planet’s mysteries, I’m sure we’ll move on.”
You ask Lessau if he knew your father, a query that prompts the chimera’s ears to flatten and his gaze to shift briefly downward.

“I knew him quite well, yes, though chiefly in the final decades. I first met him some two centuries ago, when I was a student. He came to see my professor, who like myself studied nanomedicine, to acquire his own microsurgeons. They were experimental, far less refined and far less safe than yours, but he made a convincing argument for testing them on the frontier. I remember feeling a sense of power about him that I’ve not seen since...” Lessau trails off, looking you over. “...Though perhaps I see it now.”

"+(pc.isAss()?"You stand with arms folded, tapping your foot impatiently at his assessment.":"You fidget a bit, not quite sure exactly what he’s looking for.")+" Soon enough he finishes gazing and resumes his story.

“I didn’t interact with him much at that time, of course, only incidentally. Still, he reached out to me twenty years ago, when it became clear his health was in decline. He wanted a new head of the biotechnology division of Steele Tech, and wanted me to head it.”

You ask why dad reached out to Lessau instead of his mentor, prompting a deep frown from the chimera. “Yes, well, he would have, but she was already acquired by Xenogen. They would not be willing to let her go at any price.”
“You make it sound like she’s property,” you reply. Lessau fixes you with as serious a look as you’ve ever seen from anyone.
<i>“You don’t leave Xenogen, [pc.name]. Not if they find you useful, or if you grow too knowledgeable about what they do behind closed doors.
I would caution you against doing any more for them than you already have, or you too may find yourself considered an asset too important to let roam free.
Lessau lets the statement hang, seemingly unwilling to go into more lurid detail. The moment eventually passes, and Lessau returns to his story after a moment to collect himself.

“At the time Steele Tech was mostly a mining company. The biotechnology division was woefully underdeveloped and underfunded, so we began from almost nothing. Between Victor’s generous funding and some fortunate breakthroughs, we’ve managed to develop at a brisk pace since then. We even bought the rights to a terran transformative and improved it. Sadly it did little to help Victor’s condition, but it proved the commercial viability of our department as well. Sales of Humana+ have been brisk... or Terran Treats, as I gather most people call them.” Lessau grumbles a bit at the mention of the more popular name.

“The real goal, however, was in a concept I had spent decades drafting and refining, but lacked the funding to develop until Victor’s intervention. With his aid I produced my masterwork: microsurgeons that do more than just protect against known diseases, they adapt, they innovate when faced with the unknown. More importantly, they house within them a full transcript of your original genome from birth. No matter how you alter yourself, no matter what mutations you undergo, you will always be able to return to what you once were.”
“And, of course, you went and found one of the only things in the galaxy beyond their ability to repair. I suppose I should have expected as much from a Steele.”
You just give Lessau an innocent look,
pretending not to know what he’s annoyed at
not understanding what he’s unhappy about
. After a moment the chimera gives a defeated sigh, one of his extra hands coming up to rub at the bridge of his nose.

“If, somehow, you manage to claim your fortune in that condition, given enough time I may be able to undo the damage you’ve done to yourself. I’ll need to do a hard reset on your genome, and lord knows what else might have to be done to deal with the neural patterns....” The doctor trails off into irritated mutterings before looking back up at you.
“For the foreseeable future, however, I’m afraid you’re stuck with it.”

It’s kind of a squicky question, but you can’t help but ask. You know he did it with damn near everyone else.
“So, did you and dad ever...?”
You trail off as Lessau gives a hearty laugh.

“He asked, certainly! But no, I did not. It was not that I was unwilling; your father was quite the charmer. It would have contributed further to his declining health, with the amount of mutations I’ve undergone that would have been communicable.”
“You, on the other hand...” With this the doctor’s smile turns surprisingly playful.
“You present no such issues. If you’d like to claim territory your father never could, I wouldn’t mind enjoying the fruits of your journey.”
Well, that certainly sounds like an invitation!

“So, what "+(pc.isAss()?"the hell are you supposed to be anyway":"are you, exactly")+"” you ask, looking over the massive blend of traits that go into Lessau’s chimeric form. “You look like "+(pc.isAss()?"a snake fucked an ox and then that fucked a spider or something":"one of those old Terran legends")+".”

“Oh? Oh, yes, all this.” Lessau gestures to himself with one of his hands. “I enjoy switching forms every few months, this has become one of my new favorites. It’s based off the Terran myths of the chimera, though with a few tweaks. The discovery of Mhen’ga and its naleen helped enormously with refining the synthesis, enough so to make this blend reproductively viable. But if you’re asking what I originally am, I was born as a human.”
“And the four arms?” you ask, folding your own. In response Lessau lifts his two left arms, letting you see how the paired shoulders function and move independently.

“These are a new addition. I’m testing if the Brundle Limitation can be overcome by forgoing legs. Results so far are promising...” Lessau looks over at his raised arms, which have begun mimicking each other’s actions to his visible annoyance. “...for the most part.”

“And I would be indeed, were I unprotected. Fortunately, any piece of technology as complex as yours requires a prototype. My microsurgeons are slightly less refined than yours, but they share the same general set of functions. At present I’m using them as a testbed for developing new features that I hope to be able to pass on to yours someday.”

“In fact, I’ve just recently been working on that rather interesting data you acquired from Bell-Isle Grunmann. It may be ancient technology, but the emergent intelligence properties of those microbots is quite remarkable. Imagine having the Codex and all its data held within your very bloodstream, or having the ability to store your mind as an AI while regrowing your body from the ground up, and perhaps you begin to see the possibilities. All of this will take quite some time before it’s ready to test, but your discovery promises to be a monumental advancement.”

You can only assume that Dr. Lessau was human at one point, but it’s impossible to tell under the mountain of gene mods he’s undergone, which have turned him into a creature similar to the mythic chimera. From the waist down his lower body is a massive serpent’s tail, normally spiraled into a coil underneath him to keep him at a few inches over six feet. The bulk of the tail’s scales are pitch black, save for a strip of white that runs down the front. His upper body is thickly muscled and covered in a thin layer of white fur that sports a thicker layer of orange around his shoulders and collar. Matching thick orange bands of fur are wrapped around each of his four wrists. Despite the thickness of his muscles, his hands are lean and nimble, the fingers tipped in sharp feline claws. His head is that of a lion, including an orange mane that cascades down his back as hair, but a pair of massive black horns stretch out from underneath the hair, more like a bull’s horns than a goat’s in how they angle up and forward. Adorning his back are a set of white feathered wings.
The doctor stands nude save for a lab coat worn open. He shows no obvious sexual endowments at first glance
, but you know from experience he’s packing a cock just as monstrous as he is in a well-hidden genital slit
You reach over the desk and run a finger across the fluff along Lessau’s chest.

You ask Lessau about the sealed research shaft you’ve seen. The mention of it makes Lessau noticeably nervous.
“That was sealed just before you arrived, in fact. We’re waiting on a hazard team to arrive to address the situation.”
“Oh? What situation?” you ask, your curiosity now piqued. Lessau freezes, growls at himself, then sighs.

“A short time before you arrived, there was an accident that damaged the casing of one of our deepsea facility’s power conduits. That in itself was an inconvenience, if a hazardous one to the native life. However, before it could be repaired it drew the attention of one of the native bioforms, a minor predator of small fish, that began feeding on the electricity from the exposed conduit. Within minutes it bloated to enormous size and broke into the facility to begin drawing on the generator directly. We managed to evacuate, but as we did Typhon, the station’s AI, reported that he was being attacked as well. I don’t know what kind of condition he or the facility are in now. All reports from down there have stopped.”
You ask if you can go down to check it out yourself. You are kind of in the adventuring business, after all. Lessau looks aghast, even if he’s not surprised.
“Absolutely not! We have no idea what the situation is down there, it would be insane to let you go.”
You point out that if the creature was growing that rapidly, they might not have time for the hazard team to arrive now. You’re here now, and you’re pretty undeniably badass.
You beat the frostwyrm, after all.
"+M.m.numToText(e,!0)+" times!

“Just like your father...if you’re going to go, make sure you bring some kind of thermal or freezing weaponry. The creature’s adapted to the ocean floor, it will be used to relatively stable temperatures compared to most life on this planet. I’ll authorize you for permission, but be warned that you likely won’t be able to return until you’ve dealt with the situation. Stay safe, [pc.name], I have no desire to lose you over this.”
You assure Lessau that you’ll be fine, giving him a thumbs-up as you leave his office. Time to go check out the research shaft.
You ask Lessau about the frostwyrm, the planet’s apex predator.

“Ah, those...I can’t tell you how much I’ve wanted a blood sample from them. Unfortunately, based on what native legends say of it, the blood crystallizes shortly after being exposed to the air, or on the creature’s death the moment its psionic field breaks down. We know it possesses an enormous amount of savicite in its bloodstream, and its DNA would be immediately ruined by the crystallization. In order to get a sample you would have to subdue...one...alive.”
The chimeric doctor trails off and sighs, using one of his hands to facepalm. “And now you’re going to go do it, aren’t you.”

You tell the doctor that you’ll do your best, putting the extractor away.
You place the filled extractor on Lessau’s desk as you walk in, a triumphant grin on your face. Lessau for his part looks downright stunned.

Go Hellhound

After dressing the confused deertaur in heavy, insulating clothes and covering her oversized endowments as best you can, you and Amber venture out of your ship and onto Uveto Station. Like a newborn, her curious eyes examine everything, though her good spirits turn into fear once she sees the rows of machine guns lining the walls.
“Are you sure there’s a scientist in this place?” she asks, uneasy.
“This here is just the space station,” you explain. “The person I want you to meet is on the planet proper.”

Thankfully, it takes only a few minutes for you to leave that area and reach the space elevator, but now the deertaur finds herself faced with a new challenge: the frigid winds of Uveto. The civilized area might be considered warm in comparison with the glacial wastelands that cover most of the planet, but it’s still bitterly cold to someone used to the tropical heat of Mhen’ga.
“Is this-ss-s really necess-ssarryy?” she asks, shivering visibly as you go down the space elevator and escort her through the streets.

You reassure her that it’s just a short walk; you’re almost there. Huskar and other dwellers of Uveto cast curious glances her way – it’s not common to see a ‘taur in these parts, never mind one as cute as Amber – but she’s so focused on the cold that she doesn’t even notice the more lustful looks. Good.

Soon enough, you arrive at Steele Tech’s biomedical division, and the deertaur gives a sigh of relief once the climatized air starts to warm up her reddened cheeks. As you enter Dr. Lessau’s office, the scientist gives you and her a curious look.
“Captain Steele,” he nods with a warm smile, “and... who might this marvelous lady be?”
“Uhh, hi, I guess! I’m Amber,” she introduces herself a little awkwardly, extending a hand. Dr. Lessau shakes it firmly, though his gaze seems more interested in her tauric half than anything else.
“I’m Lessau, head of research. Pray, forgive my curiosity, but were you always like this?” he asks, gesturing to her deer body.
Amber winces a little. “Oh, that’s kind of a long story. I don’t want to take too much of your time...”
You decide to chime in: “That’s actually why we’re here, so you might as well give him the full version.”

The deertaur spends a few minutes telling the chimera about her misadventures, how she wound up with her current body and her off the charts libido. Dr. Lessau takes notes at several points, stopping Amber to ask for details about the kinds of transformatives she used – how long the changes took to manifest, whether the people she got the drugs from mentioned anything of relevance, everything from how they looked to how she felt. Amber strains to recall as much as possible, but most of her memories from those times are little more than a haze.

When it becomes apparent that there’s nothing else to be gained from this, you stop the interrogation and explain that you’re looking for a way to cure her condition. Dr. Lessau helped your father before, so surely he might be able to help you...
“Cure?” the scientist asks with a raised eyebrow. “I’m afraid you’ll have to be a little more specific, Captain. Do you mean turning her back into a human? Selectively undoing some of the changes?”

You look at Amber. She hesitates, biting her lower lip. Clearly, she wants you to make the big decisions for her, but this time you’re having none of that. After all, it’s her body and mind that we’re talking about. After a moment of awkward silence, she gathers her courage.

“I, uhh, kind of like how my body is now. I wouldn’t mind going back to human if that’s what it takes, but to be honest I just want to... to... have a little more time for myself, you know. Without this,” she blushes, “this constant need to fuck. Like, I just want a few more hours per day to focus on stuff other than sex.”
The scientist looks at his clock, then nods. “Okay, I’ve got some time before I need to continue my current experiments. Let’s go down to the lab so we can get a proper look at your genes, shall we?”
An hour later...

Dr. Lessau sighs. “This is a mess. You see, Amber, the mods didn’t just raise your libido to extraordinary levels; they put you in heat permanently. What you think is your heat phase is actually just a cyclic peak in your hormonal curve. A small deviation in the grand scale of things.”
“How much?” you ask, already feeling the hole in your bank account.

The chimera waves his hands dismissively. “It’s not just about credits, it’s about manpower and facilities. Steele Tech’s biodivision works on a tight schedule as it is – I can’t afford to divert this lab’s resources, much as I would like to. I was hoping it would be something I could work on as a pet project, but the damage is so extensive that it would take a custom build of nanomachines to achieve anything of significance.”
Amber looks crestfallen. Even Dr. Lessau seems to feel the sting of her dejection, judging by the look of pity on his face.

“However, the situation is not without hope,” he amends, trying to raise her spirits. “Since you don’t require any overt changes to your body, it might be achievable in a shorter time frame, and while I cannot allocate this laboratory to your cause, I can share the data I have.”
Amber perks up at that, but you give Dr. Lessau a pointed look. “So, you’re telling us to look for someone else.”
“Technically, yes,” the scientist admits with a sigh.
“And do you have any suggestions?” you ask.

He shakes his head despondently. “No, unfortunately not. You’d want someone unaffiliated with any of the other major corporations, but all of my colleagues have already been taken by the likes of Xenogen, Tamani Corp, and Snugglé.”
So, basically what Dr. Lessau is suggesting is that you look for an independent scientist, someone without ties to the major corps...
Well, you happen to know a certain orange gabilani that fits the bill. Despite her unpredictable antics, you have a feeling she and Amber would get along pretty well. Perhaps a little too well.
You have a hunch you might find such an individual on Tarkus, what with all the technological junk lying around. Let’s see...
Dr. Badger? No, that person would only make Amber’s condition worse.
Besides, she is in prison.
Dr. Lash? Hell no, you don’t want to sterilize the poor deer.
There’s got to be an independent scientist who’s not totally crazy...
Looks like you’ll have to scour some Rush planets for such an individual. Chances of finding them on one of the more well-established regions are slim, at best.

After another minute of awkward conversation, Dr. Lessau apologizes once again for not being able to help directly, but he reassures you that he will share the relevant data, if you find a trustworthy scientist to carry out the research proper.
Well, time to start looking...

Steele Tech’s biomedical division has a cozy office feel to it, white walls and dark wood paneling complementing the dark blue carpeting. Other researchers are off in cubicles along the walls that provide a bit more privacy. Bookcases dot the walls; most are filled with textbooks, but you see a few shelves dedicated to tabletop gamebooks. To the north, [frostwyrm.andNykke |the dune that Nykke was staring at |a tall snow dune, with its crest reaching higher than your head ] begins to shift: snow cascades down its sloped sides in thin sheets, piling up at the dune’s base as something inside it begins to stir. An elongated section of snow shifts and lifts into the air, reminding you almost of a leg with a stout, arrowhead-shaped foot – until you make out telltale horns with snow piling on top of their crooks, framing a long, thin snout beneath them. It seems you’ve found your Frostwyrm. As if to announce itself, its furled wings slowly peel from its body before violently flapping open, flinging snow in a bright, dusty cloud away from it as it shakes its body clean of all the remaining snow still clinging to its scales. With almost belabored and frustrated movements, its six legs lift out from underneath itself, one after the other, stamping into the snow and sinking down to where its ankles would be with each heavy limb. It’s too far away for you to see its eyes, but you can see its head, and you can tell that it’s looking directly at you[frostwyrm.andNykke |, Nykke, ] and [frostwyrm.name]. Before it makes its way towards you, you hear a long, deep, bellowing noise coming from what you presume to be its throat – a sort of lyrical sound that starts at one note, then smoothly transitions to a higher note, before ending suddenly on a lower note than it started with. Its massive wings beat against the snowy tundra as it puts one foot in front of the other five, walking, then cantering, then galloping towards you, using its wings to accelerate. You feel a bit exposed, standing in front of a charging Frostwyrm like this. [pc.isAss |But you hold your ground: you had bested [frostwyrm.name] in combat, after all, and you don’t want to appear weak in the face of another Frostwyrm, especially in front of her. |You had beaten [frostwyrm.name] in combat multiple times, but she never approached you with intent like this. A part of you wants to try and at least get out of the way or something – but you feel something in your psyche. Something calming and reassuring; something that straightens your back and heightens your focus. [frostwyrm.name] is with you, and she’d never let anything happen to you. ] "])))

textify(Tr||(Tr=(0,C.Z)([" The second Frostwyrm approaches with thunderous stomps, the snow exploding around its feet every time they slam down. Just as you start to think that it’s not going to stop and you brace for impact, it plants four of its six feet into the snow and skids to a halt in front of you [frostwyrm.andNykke |three |two ], kicking up a wave of snow that only just manages to reach your ankles by the time it comes to a full rest. Before you stands a second Frostwyrm, just as you were anticipating. It is, in most ways, somewhat identical to [frostwyrm.name], in the same way that you’d be identical to another [pc.race] with the same color of [pc.skinFurScalesNoun]: white scales cover its upper body, with its softer, more malleable scales darkened to a deeper grey. Each of its six legs are capped with razor-sharp talons that could cut perfect lines through the ice. Its long, thick tail is capped with blunt spikes dotting its tip, better for bludgeoning than for piercing. Blue streaks dress its white scales all over, appearing almost as tattoos. But they’re all subtly different. The spikes on its tail are misshapen: some of them are shorter than others, and one of them is missing entirely. Its scales, particularly along the membranes of its wings, are pockmarked with grey scars, with some of them looking like they’ve been opened more than once. Chinks have been taken out of the talons on its feet, giving them an unnaturally serrated look. And the blue swirls on its body are significantly different from [frostwyrm.name]’s: rather than looking like swirls with stems, they look more like tree branches, with the patterns forking into multiple branches, and not all of them appear symmetrical – although it’s hard to tell if that’s because they’re hidden by scars. Its four red eyes train on [frostwyrm.name] with a familiar kind of intensity: it reminds you of when you and [frostwyrm.name] didn’t know each other, and she approached you with combative intent. Although there’s something different about this one’s posture: where [frostwyrm.name] came at you with wings spread and talons pointed forward, this one approaches with a shimmy in its hind hips and awkward posing in its upper half. Thick, low trills escape its throat occasionally as it regards [frostwyrm.name]. It almost looks like a mating dance. [frostwyrm.name], however, remains nonplussed: she sits on her haunches, her wings folded regally behind herself, watching this second Frostwyrm dance and jiggle in front of her like she was a queen watching a jester underperform in her court. Whatever noise or motion the second Frostwyrm makes, she remains wholly unimpressed and she doesn’t reciprocate any of them. After a moment of this, the second Frostwyrm stops, staring into [frostwyrm.name]’s eyes for a moment. You hear [frostwyrm.name] shift beside you, her head craning downwards to put herself beside you, but her eyes never leave the second Frostwyrm’s[frostwyrm.andNykke |. And, after a moment, she does the same to Nykke, leaning her body closer to yours so she can reach Nykke’s standing form ]. This must be [frostwyrm.name] introducing you[frostwyrm.andNykke | both ]. They will approach you, my Qal, as I had approached you, you hear her say – and she puts a weird amount of emphasis on the word ‘Qal.’ Let them. The second Frostwyrm’s eyes have gone from [frostwyrm.name]’s to yours. At first, it looks at you as though it didn’t notice you were there to begin with... but the change in body language is sudden and distinct. Its four red eyes stay on you, their lids widened, watching you for even the slightest twitch in your body. Its wings flex and strain, locked in a half-furled position, ready to pounce into the air at a moment’s notice. For all the world, it looks like it’s one step away from thrashing at you. [pc.isAss|Let it; you’re not scared. You’ve handled a Frostwyrm before and you’ll do it again.|You’ve handled Frostwyrms in combat before, and you’re ready to do it again... but you’re glad to have [frostwyrm.name] on your side. Beating her in a fight wasn’t exactly easy, after all.] The second Frostwyrm leans forward, its neck outstretched towards you, its pointed face closing the gap until it’s roughly a foot away from you. With an exhale through its flared nostrils, its steamy breath washes over your form, and, just like with [frostwyrm.name][frostwyrm.andNykke | and Nykke ], you feel a strange tightness in your head and you see a throbbing in your vision – before everything dissipates and you see as clearly as you had before. Whelp, you hear a voice say – one utterly distinct from [frostwyrm.andNykke |either [frostwyrm.name] or Nykke |[frostwyrm.name] ], echoing in your head and bouncing between your ears. Its voice is low and powerful, while [frostwyrm.name]’s is slightly higher and more sonant. If you were to describe them as instruments, [frostwyrm.name]’s reminds you of an oboe, a smooth but low-pitched wind instrument, while this Frostwyrm’s voice reminds you of a war drum. You have laid claim to– Its voice bursts into a number of syllables consisting of hard consonants and trilling contractions that your mouth could never hope to reproduce. You recognize it as [frostwyrm.name]’s true name – this second Frostwyrm is confirming that you are [frostwyrm.name]’s mate. And you say that you have: you are [frostwyrm.andNykke |Qal not only to [frostwyrm.name], but to Nykke, as well |[frostwyrm.name]’s Qal. A relationship that she initially proposed ]. Its eyes pull away from you to lift back up to [frostwyrm.name]’s. Her posture has returned to the regal stance it was before: on her haunches, her back straightened to the point where she is looking down at it, perhaps deliberately. This is a mockery, it says. A jest. A canard. You, a Qim to... Its eyes go back to you, its snout curling inward so that its scales fold over themselves. It’s very close to bearing its fangs. This. With your mind tethered to this second Frostwyrm, you can feel the emotions flooding from it, unbidden, into your own psyche. There are a number of distinct feelings you can make out, including anger at the revelation and frustration that it had let itself get excited enough to immediately proposition [frostwyrm.name] with a mating dance. But, more than those, you feel... despondence. Despair. Where once there was loneliness and hollowness, there was, for a fleeting moment, hope that shone brightly and fiercely, once it had found another of its kind, only for that flame to instantly be snuffed when it learned that she was spoken for. By you. [pc.ra Frostwyrm |A shallow, insulting mockery of what a proper Frostwyrm ought to be. Not only did you claim the only viable mate on this part of the planet, but you’ve done it by appropriating her very body for yourself |Not even another Frostwyrm; [frostwyrm.name] was driven so desperate for her need of a mate that she would debase herself by taking... whatever the hell it is you even are as a Qal ][frostwyrm.andNykke |. And not only [frostwyrm.name]: you had taken a half-breed into your clutch as well. And [frostwyrm.name] encouraged it! Here you are with two mates, both of them pumping Frostwyrm blood, and it with none ]. The emotions flooding into you change: at first, frustration; then, despair; and now, jealousy and spite. Its four red eyes stare at you with a certain darkness in their difficult-to-see pupils; its lids pull back far enough that you can nearly see the bend of their circumferences in their sockets. That sort of look reminds you not of a crazed animal, but a crazed person, ready to attack. You will calm yourself, [frostwyrm.name] says. A soothing sensation floods your body, starting at your shoulders and working its way down; as soon as she says the words, you feel yourself automatically respond. But the words clearly weren’t meant for you: they were meant for the other Frostwyrm, and the effect her words have are visible, but not nearly as well pronounced. Act as though you are under the eye of the Grand Qim. Hypocrite! the second Frostwyrm barks, lifting its head and opening its jaw to gutturally howl at [frostwyrm.name]. As though the Grand Qim would beseech you take a mongrel for a mate! The Grand Qim would not see us turn on each other in our mutual times of scarcity, [frostwyrm.name] answers, her composure as rock-steady as ever. Quell your frustrations. Our intent was to come to your aid. It has been innumerable Sky Cycles since either of us had seen another of our kind. Allow us to converse before you act. The second Frostwyrm flares its nostrils, its breath coming out ragged as though it had just come in from a heavy sprint. With a long, throaty sigh that starts rough but ends smoothly, it eventually relents, assuming a similar posture to [frostwyrm.name]: sittings on its haunches, its back straight, its wings furled up at its sides. But for all the calmness it’s trying to exude, there is an obvious line of irate impatience flowing from its psionic link into you. Converse, then, it grumbles. A low, echoing growl emanates from its throat. Its snout is pointed towards [frostwyrm.name], but its eyes are focused solely on you. It’s willing to talk, you can tell that that’s sincere – but you can’t be sure it’s willing to be reasoned with. "])))

textify(Ir||(Ir=(0,C.Z)([" It’s a fact, as unfortunate as it is, that the Frostwyrms are going extinct. It was no small miracle that you had found [frostwyrm.name] at all, and you[frostwyrm.andNykke | three | and her ] only found the second Frostwyrm after hearing rumors that it existed at all. [frostwyrm.name] frequently reminds you that you weren’t her first choice as her mate, and she only chose you because you had proven yourself a viable mate. I already have queries, the Frostwyrm interrupts. Who or what is [frostwyrm.name]? Your mouth can’t pronounce [frostwyrm.name]’s true name – hell, with how you intrinsically understand languages, your brain can hardly do it – so she had resolved to allowing you to give her a name that you could pronounce. And you thought [frostwyrm.name] was a fitting name for her. The Frostwyrm snorts, its nostril crinkling along its muzzle as before, looking for all the world that it had just smelt a rancid pile of shit in front of it. Second, it continues, ‘she’ and ‘her’ – what are these words you insist on using? [frostwyrm.name] answers before you can. The [pc.race] mind, while labyrinthian, can be explored as ours can. Their languages are at once wanting and lacking, yet nuanced and specific, compared and contrasted to our own. If there are words you do not understand, you may explore my Qal’s thoughts for their definitions. Then, with emphasis, she adds, and nothing else. Unbidden, you feel the familiar pressure in your mind as [frostwyrm.name] scours your thoughts for your memories – and then, a second, heavier, and more directionless pressure is added on top of hers, giving you the wholly unpleasant sensation of your brain being too heavy for your skull. The second pressure ‘feels’ different: it’s similar to the different texture between sand and powdered snow, where one is coarser and less free-flowing than the other[frostwyrm.andNykke |. You’ve had more than one Frostwyrm in your head before – and, during sex, the mind-melding can get pretty intense – but both [frostwyrm.name] and Nykke had experience reading your mind up until that point. This second Frostwyrm intrudes into your thoughts the same way a battering ram intrudes into a door |; you remember [frostwyrm.name] not being especially gentle with her mind reading when she first started out, too. Hopefully this one just needs a bit of teaching to get used to it ]. Eventually, after having its fill rummaging through your head for your memories, you feel one of the pressures, [frostwyrm.name]’s, leave your head. The second one takes a moment longer to leave... or, perhaps, [frostwyrm.name] forcefully pulled it out. It is a wonder of the world that kinds like yours exist with such unfocused thoughts, the second Frostwyrm thrums. It lowers its head towards you, its snout bumping into your chest, not cruelly, but in an attempt to size you up. And yet you are the Qal. The duty of the Qal is to pass on the knowledge and teachings of prior generations onto the kips. Despite my misgivings, I assume this is not a mistake. You had said earlier that [frostwyrm.name] chose you because you had proven yourself to be a viable mate. She only came to that conclusion after you had defeated her. Multiple times. In single combat. From the tone in its voice, it doesn’t believe you, but, yes. You did it alone. The second Frostwyrm says nothing. It sits back onto its haunches, mimicking [frostwyrm.name]’s posture once again, and turns its red eyes towards her, looking to her to confirm what it clearly thinks is an absolutely outrageous claim. After all, it is true that [frostwyrm.name] is several times your height, and many multiple times your weight. It is true, she confirms. [pc.name] is much smaller than us. I had reasoned that any [pc.hasCock |virile[pc.isHerm | and fertile ] |fertile ] creature that was capable of downing a being like myself so consistently had a far greater understanding of the world than I possessed, and would therefore make an excellent Qal. A light, trilling thrum emanates from her chest. My conclusion has been correct so far.[frostwyrm.andNykke | The second Frostwyrm turns its gaze towards Nykke, standing notably shorter than [frostwyrm.name][pc.heightRange 0 108 |, although still significantly taller than you ]. And you? it asks, its tone halfway between exasperation and disbelief in the scenario it’s found itself in. Are you the same? Did you challenge this creature to single combat and lose badly enough that you chose to mate with it? Nykke twists her mouth and furrows her brow at its choice in words. “The truth is that I met our Qal in the snowbanks where [frostwyrm.name] was brooding, just like you had,” she answers verbally. “Our kind is dying out. I had the choice of either joining their clutch and becoming a second Qim to our Qal, or to keep wandering the world in the hopes that I’d find another viable mate for myself.” She turns her head to one side and lowers her eyelids. “The same choice you’re facing right now.” Our plights are not the same, it answers, the edges of its scaly mouth curving upward in disdain. “I can tell that you’ve seen a lot of fighting, but from the layers on your horns and talons, you’re not as old as either me or [frostwyrm.name],” Nykke says back. Despite the conviction in her words, she speaks as though she’s working through a lump in her throat. “We’ve been looking for mates longer than you have. We know exactly what you’re going through.” ] The second Frostwyrm turns its eyes to you, then to [frostwyrm.name][frostwyrm.andNykke |, then to Nykke, then back to [frostwyrm.name] one final time ].[frostwyrm.andNykke | You are suggesting I do what you two have done, it concludes. To join your clutch as a third Qim to this apparent specimen. It is an unusual arrangement, [frostwyrm.name] concedes. There were initial difficulties. Yet the alternative is to continue to weigh your odds against the endless expanse of the world to find a mate for yourself. | What is it you are suggesting, then? it asks, its patience beginning to wear thin in its tone. Unless the purpose of your finding me is to mock me that this neophyte has what I cannot have. [frostwyrm.name] takes a long, deep sigh through her nose, her massive lungs flexing with all of that air inside them. Like it as not, the world is vacant of our kind, she answers. We are not given the luxury of options, nor the fortune of plenty as our bearers once did. Our kind take only one mate, yet... we have been given a challenge, and, by the grace of the Grand Qim or not, we have been given a solution. One that will be initially uncomfortable for us both. The second Frostwyrm tilts its head in confusion at what [frostwyrm.name] could mean... before its eyes suddenly widen in understanding. ] I would be neither Qim nor Qal to my own clutch, the second Frostwyrm says, bereaved. I would all but be given the title of Qer. A scrounger – a parasite, depending on the charity of a kind that has been claimed, and the apparent knowledge of this cross-breeding interloper you have brought before me.[frostwyrm.andNykke | You feel the anger flare from Nykke through your connection to her before you hear her words or see her actions. “You need to shut the fuck up,” Nykke gnashes suddenly, lifting her upper right arm and pointing a finger at the second Frostwyrm. “My own Qal was a ‘cross-breeder,’ too, and if I’m not a Qim to [pc.name], that makes me a Qer, according to you. And I am not a Qer. You’re sitting there, thinking you’re too good for me or [pc.name], but from where we are, you’re the one without a clutch or a mate and you’re the one getting upset at the idea of having anything if it means sharing.” You lift your hand to Nykke’s, gently resting your palm on her raised hand to get her to lower it. The less tensions there are in the air, the better. She lowers her hand – eventually, and slowly. The second Frostwyrm’s posture remains static and difficult to read, but you can at least tell that it’s watching Nykke and listening to her words. “I hunt; I bring food back to the home; I’ve made myself a lair; and I raise the kips that we have reared together. I’m as much a proper Qim as [frostwyrm.name] is. It’s been Sky Cycles since any of us has seen another of our kind; if you want to have any sort of closure in your life, you need to accept that you might never–” Nykke catches herself, unwilling to finish the thought. It’s not a truth that she wants to face, and as soon as the last word hangs in the air, you can feel it from both [frostwyrm.name] and the second Frostwyrm as well: uncomfortable apprehension. Knowing that there’s a truth they must face – a line they must cross – but they’re focusing on anything and everything else to keep from facing it. | Cease with the vile rhetoric! [frostwyrm.name]’s voice bellows in your head. For the first time since you had started, her posture breaks: the hackles of her foremost limbs breach upward and she lowers her head, looking as though she’s readying herself to lunge, if it weren’t for her lowered rump. There is no worthier being in this world than [pc.name] as my Qal. We had come in search of you to offer a respite from the ugly reality of our predicament. It is no small sacrifice on my part to offer you a place in my nest – near our clutch, need I remind you! The barest of compliments you will yield is the respect we are due! ] Everyone’s been taking the conversation in a very roundabout manner so far, so, although you realize the tensions are high and you’re not exactly the second Frostwyrm’s role model, you try to boil down the situation as best as you can: there aren’t a lot of Frostwyrms left on Uveto. You had taken [frostwyrm.name][frostwyrm.andNykke | and Nykke as mates | as a mate ] and you had raised a[var0 | | small ] clutch with [frostwyrm.andNykke |them |her ] before you had realized that there was a second Frostwyrm wandering the plains nearby. You realize that this isn’t how Frostwyrms do things, and that it’s hurt and confused and desperate to have a normal life with a normal family, but the Frostwyrms are facing a harsh reality right now. You[frostwyrm.andNykke |, Nykke, ] and [frostwyrm.name] are willing to take it into your family and give it a home. And give it any other accommodations it might require. The second Frostwyrm remains still for a moment before eventually rotating its head to one side. A clutch? it asks. We had spoken of mates, and Qims and Qals, and combat prowess, though this is the first time offspring has been mentioned[frostwyrm.andNykke |. Though I suppose I should have assumed interbreeding was possible, given the third party ]. Its red eyes fixate on you and it leans forward, its snout coming within a foot from your body. All four red eyes focus on you, but on different parts of you, with the eyeballs moving to scan different limbs for any sudden movements. Allow me to see what you know of clutches. You feel the familiar cloying in your mind, of an alien presence trying to force its way into your memories. It’s as unrefined as it was previously: intent, vehement, and without any technique. It’s as uncomfortable as it was before and you can’t help but wince and reach for your head. As soon as you do, though, you feel [frostwyrm.andNykke |both Nykke and ][frostwyrm.name]’s presence alongside it, trying to guide it and refine it so that it’s not nearly as intense. It helps; the presence in your mind becomes more focused and specific, but still without any finesse, trying to force its way through you. Your linguistic skills need improvement, [frostwyrm.name] chastises, but does not try to force the second Frostwyrm out of your head like she had before. "]))),[function(){return!!flags.FROSTWYRM_KIP_COUNT&&flags.FROSTWYRM_KIP_COUNT>5}]
